skill.name: data-quality-frameworks
skill.description: Guides implementation of data quality checks using Great Expectations, dbt tests, data contracts, and pipeline reporting patterns.
skill.summary: Build practical data quality validation for analytics and pipeline workflows.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples, inline framework names, schema metadata, and resource links rather than executable malicious code. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because it includes user-facing shell setup commands and outbound documentation links that users may choose to follow.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported external command locations are Markdown code fences, inline expectation names, or example setup commands for Great Expectations and dbt. No file contains executable hooks, hidden scripts, command interpolation, or automatic shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is SKILL.md, and the flagged locations are documentation snippets. There is no executable script or runtime path that would run these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines describe data quality dimensions, table descriptions, identifiers, timestamps, and documentation text. No evidence found of cryptographic API usage, weak hash selection, encryption, decryption, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found data modeling and validation text at the reported locations. The scanner appears to have matched generic terms rather than real cryptographic behavior.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Data Validation Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system and network reconnaissance hits occur in dbt model references, SQL validation examples, UUID or timestamp schema fields, and accepted values examples. They do not inspect the host, enumerate networks, or collect environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are part of data quality sample logic rather than host or network discovery. No system commands, sockets, scanners, or filesystem enumeration are present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The four hardcoded URLs point to public documentation for Great Expectations, dbt, data contracts, and Soda Core. They are not called by code and do not transmit user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in the Resources section of the Markdown file. No network client code or data exfiltration path exists in the scanned file.

content.value_statement: Data teams need reliable checks before bad data reaches analytics or production workflows. This skill provides practical patterns for Great Expectations, dbt tests, contracts, and quality reports.
actual_capabilities: Explains core data quality dimensions such as completeness, uniqueness, validity, consistency, and timeliness.
Provides Great Expectations setup and expectation suite examples for common warehouse tables.
Shows dbt schema tests, generic tests, singular tests, and relationship checks.
Outlines data contract structure with schema, ownership, PII flags, quality checks, and SLAs.
Demonstrates a Python validation pipeline that runs checks and generates a quality report.
limitations: Does not include runnable project files beyond Markdown examples.
Requires users to adapt table names, thresholds, and schemas to their warehouse.
Does not replace security, privacy, or compliance review for production data contracts.
Assumes familiarity with Python, dbt, SQL, and warehouse validation concepts.

use_cases.title: Add Warehouse Quality Checks
use_cases.description: Create validation coverage for critical marts with uniqueness, not-null, range, freshness, and relationship checks.

use_cases.title: Define Producer Consumer Contracts
use_cases.description: Document schema fields, owners, PII handling, quality checks, and service expectations for shared data products.

use_cases.title: Automate Pipeline Failure Rules
use_cases.description: Use validation results to generate reports and fail workflows when important tables do not meet expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Quality Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a data quality checklist for my orders table. Include completeness, uniqueness, validity, and freshness checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design dbt Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to propose dbt tests for these models and columns. Explain which tests belong at the table level and column level.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Data Contract
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a data contract for this dataset. Include owner, schema fields, PII flags, quality rules, and SLA expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Validation Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an automated validation workflow for multiple warehouse tables. Include failure handling, reporting, and CI integration.

output_examples.input: Create quality checks for an orders fact table.
output: A table-level freshness check for recent order creation timestamps.
Column checks for unique order identifiers and non-null customer identifiers.
Accepted status values and positive amount validation.
A relationship check from orders to the customer dimension.

output_examples.input: Draft a data contract for customer events.
output: A contract outline with owner, version, purpose, and usage limits.
Schema fields with required flags, type expectations, and PII classification.
Quality checks for row count, missing identifiers, valid event types, and freshness.
Service expectations for availability, freshness, and latency.

output_examples.input: Plan a validation pipeline for three warehouse tables.
output: A run order for table validation suites.
A result summary with passed and failed expectations.
Failure rules that stop the pipeline when critical checks fail.
A short report format for operations and analytics stakeholders.

best_practices: Start with critical columns and business rules before expanding coverage.
Use dynamic thresholds for row counts and freshness when data volume changes often.
Version data contracts and validation rules with the pipeline code that depends on them.
anti_patterns: Testing every column without prioritizing business impact.
Ignoring freshness and relationship checks when source tables arrive late or incomplete.
Hardcoding thresholds that create noise or miss real data quality changes.

faq.question: Does this skill run Great Expectations or dbt automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples that users adapt and run in their own projects.

faq.question: Can it help with data contracts?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows contract sections for metadata, schema, quality rules, PII classification, and SLA expectations.

faq.question: Is this only for analytics warehouses?
faq.answer: No. The examples focus on warehouse workflows, but the validation concepts also apply to pipelines and shared data products.

faq.question: Does it include production-ready thresholds?
faq.answer: No. Thresholds are examples and should be calibrated against real data behavior and business risk.

faq.question: Which tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Great Expectations, dbt tests, data contracts, Soda-style checks, and Python validation orchestration.

faq.question: What should users provide before using it?
faq.answer: Users should provide table names, key columns, expected values, freshness requirements, owners, and failure handling rules.
